subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Consider a light beam incident from air to a glass slab at Brewster’s angle as shown in Figure. A polaroid is placed in the path of the emergent ray at point P and rotated about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the polaroid.
question_html: Consider a light beam incident from air to a glass slab at Brewster’s angle as shown in Figure. A polaroid is placed in the path of the emergent ray at point P and rotated about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the polaroid.

options.text: For a particular orientation there shall be darkness as observed through the polaoid
options.html: For a particular orientation there shall be darkness as observed through the polaoid

options.text: The intensity of light as seen through the polaroid shall be independent of the rotation
options.html: The intensity of light as seen through the polaroid shall be independent of the rotation

options.text: The intensity of light as seen through the Polaroid shall go through a minimum but not zero for two orientations of the polaroid
options.html: The intensity of light as seen through the Polaroid shall go through a minimum but not zero for two orientations of the polaroid
options.explanation: Brewster’s law: Brewster discovered that when a beam of unpolarized light is reflected from a transparent medium (refractive index = µ), the reflected light is completely plane polarized at a certain angle of incidence When ray ABCD of light passes through a prism in such a way that angle between reflected ray BE and refracted ray BC is 90 o then only reflected ray is plane-polarized. So Polaroid rotated in the way of CD the intensity will never be zero but varies in one complete rotation.

options.text: The intensity of light as seen through the polaroid shall go through a minimum for four orientations of the polaroid
options.html: The intensity of light as seen through the polaroid shall go through a minimum for four orientations of the polaroid

correct_option_text: The intensity of light as seen through the Polaroid shall go through a minimum but not zero for two orientations of the polaroid

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Consider sunlight incident on a slit of width 10 4 A. The image seen through the slit shall
question_html: Consider sunlight incident on a slit of width 104 A. The image seen through the slit shall

options.text: be a fine sharp slit white in colour at the center
options.html: be a fine sharp slit white in colour at the center
options.explanation: Width of slit 10 4 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} = 10,000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} Wavelength of visible light varies from 4000 to 8000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} . As the width of slit 10000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} is comparable to that of wavelength of visible light i.e. 8000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} . Hence the diffraction occurs with maxima at the centre. So at the centre all colours appear ,mixing of colors forms white colour at the centre .

options.text: a bright slit white at the center diffusing to zero intensities at the edges
options.html: a bright slit white at the center diffusing to zero intensities at the edges

options.text: a bright slit white at the center diffusing to regions of different colours
options.html: a bright slit white at the center diffusing to regions of different colours

options.text: only be a diffused slit white in colour
options.html: only be a diffused slit white in colour

correct_option_text: be a fine sharp slit white in colour at the center

question_text: Consider a ray of light incident from air onto a slab of glass (refractive index n) of width d, at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} . The phase difference between the ray reflected by the top surface of the glass and the bottom surface is
question_html: Consider a ray of light incident from air onto a slab of glass (refractive index n) of width d, at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex}. The phase difference between the ray reflected by the top surface of the glass and the bottom surface is

options.text: {tex}\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\left(1-\frac{1}{n^{2}} \sin ^{2} \theta\right)^{1 / 2}+\pi{/tex}

options.explanation: The ray reflected by the top surface of the glass and the bottom surface is:- {tex}\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\left(1-\frac{1}{n^{2}} \sin ^{2} \theta\right)^{1 / 2}+\pi{/tex}

options.text: {tex}\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\left(1-\frac{1}{n^{2}} \sin ^{2} \theta\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex}

options.text: {tex}\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\left(1-\frac{1}{n^{2}} \sin ^{2} \theta\right)^{1 / 2}+\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: In Young’s double-slit experiment, the source is white light. One of the holes is covered by a red filter and another by a blue filter. In this case
question_html: In Young’s double-slit experiment, the source is white light. One of the holes is covered by a red filter and another by a blue filter. In this case

options.text: there shall be alternate interference patterns of red and blue
options.html: there shall be alternate interference patterns of red and blue

options.text: there shall be an interference pattern for red distinct from that for blue
options.html: there shall be an interference pattern for red distinct from that for blue

options.text: there shall be no interference fringes
options.html: there shall be no interference fringes
options.explanation: For sustained interference, the source must be coherent and should emit the light of same frequency. In the Young Double Slit experiment, one hole is covered with red and other with blue, which has different frequency, so no interference takes place.

options.text: there shall be an interference pattern for red mixing with one for blue
options.html: there shall be an interference pattern for red mixing with one for blue

correct_option_text: there shall be no interference fringes

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Figure shows a standard two slit arrangement with slits S 1 , S 2 . P 1 , P 2 are the two minima points on either side of P (Figure). At P 2 on the screen, there is a hole and behind P 2 is a second 2- slit arrangement with slits S 3 , S 4 and a second screen behind them.
question_html:
Figure shows a standard two slit arrangement with slits S1, S2. P1, P2 are the two minima points on either side of P (Figure).

At P2 on the screen, there is a hole and behind P2 is a second 2- slit arrangement with slits S3, S4 and a second screen behind them.

options.text: There would be no interference pattern on the second screen but it would be lighted.
options.html: There would be no interference pattern on the second screen but it would be lighted.

options.text: The second screen would be totally dark.
options.html: The second screen would be totally dark.

options.text: There would be a single bright point on the second screen.
options.html: There would be a single bright point on the second screen.

options.text: There would be a regular two slit pattern on the second screen.
options.html: There would be a regular two slit pattern on the second screen.
options.explanation: As there is a hole at minima point P 2 , the hole will act as a source of fresh light for the slits S 3 and S 4 . So, there will be a regular two slit pattern on the second screen.

correct_option_text: There would be a regular two slit pattern on the second screen.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Two sources S 1 and S 2 of intensity I 1 and I 2 are placed in front of a screen [Figure (a)]. The pattern of intensity distribution seen in the central portion is given by Figure (b). In this case which of the following statements are true. S 1 and S 2 have the same intensities. S 1 and S 2 have a constant phase difference. S 1 and S 2 have the same phase. S 1 and S 2 have the same wavelength.
question_html:
Two sources S1 and S2 of intensity I1 and I2 are placed in front of a screen [Figure (a)]. The pattern of intensity distribution seen in the central portion is given by Figure (b).
In this case which of the following statements are true.

  1. S1 and S2 have the same intensities.
  2. S1 and S2 have a constant phase difference.
  3. S1 and S2 have the same phase.
  4. S1 and S2 have the same wavelength.
solution_text: (a), (b) and (c) are correct. As the intensity at dark fringe is zero so intensities of S 1 and S 2 are equal. As the graph of maxima and minima is symmetric. So the waves from S 1 and S 2 are at same phase difference or zero phase difference.
solution_html:
(a), (b) and (c) are correct.

  1. As the intensity at dark fringe is zero so intensities of S1 and S2 are equal.
  2. As the graph of maxima and minima is symmetric. So the waves from S1 and S2 are at same phase difference or zero phase difference.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Consider sunlight incident on a pinhole of width {tex}{10^3}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} . The image of the pinhole seen on a screen shall be a sharp white ring. different from a geometrical image. a diffused central spot, white in colour. the diffused coloured region around a sharp central white spot.
question_html:
Consider sunlight incident on a pinhole of width {tex}{10^3}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}. The image of the pinhole seen on a screen shall be

  1. a sharp white ring.
  2. different from a geometrical image.
  3. a diffused central spot, white in colour.
  4. the diffused coloured region around a sharp central white spot.
solution_text: (b) and (d) are correct. The width of pinhole {tex}{10^3}\mathop A\limits^o = 1000\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} and wavelength of visible light is 4000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} to 8000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} i.e., size of slit less than (or comparable) with the wavelength of light. So light from pinhole will diffract from the hole. Due to the diffraction pattern of fringes, the shape is quite different from the hole.
solution_html:
(b) and (d) are correct.
The width of pinhole {tex}{10^3}\mathop A\limits^o = 1000\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} and wavelength of visible light is 4000{tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} to 8000{tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} i.e., size of slit less than (or comparable) with the wavelength of light.
So light from pinhole will diffract from the hole. Due to the diffraction pattern of fringes, the shape is quite different from the hole.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Consider the diffraction pattern for a small pinhole. As the size of the hole is increased the size decreases. the intensity increases. the size increases. the intensity decreases.
question_html:
Consider the diffraction pattern for a small pinhole. As the size of the hole is increased

  1. the size decreases.
  2. the intensity increases.
  3. the size increases.
  4. the intensity decreases.
solution_text: (a) and (b) are correct. We know that width (B 0 ) of central maxima {tex}\mathrm{B}_{0}=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} and width of nth secondary maxima = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} here distance (D) between slit and screen, the wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} of source does not change. So on increasing width of hole of pinhole, 'd' increase. Hence the size of central maxima decreases verifies the option (a). As the energy passing through the hole increased on increasing the size of the hole. So the intensity of pattern will increase. Hence verifies the option (b).
solution_html:
(a) and (b) are correct.
We know that width (B0) of central maxima {tex}\mathrm{B}_{0}=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} and width of nth secondary maxima = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} here distance (D) between slit and screen, the wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} of source does not change.
So on increasing width of hole of pinhole, 'd' increase. Hence the size of central maxima decreases verifies the option (a).
As the energy passing through the hole increased on increasing the size of the hole. So the intensity of pattern will increase. Hence verifies the option (b).

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: For light diverging from a point source, the wavefront is spherical. the intensity decreases in proportion to the distance squared. the wavefront is parabolic. the intensity at the wavefront does not depend on the distance.
question_html:
For light diverging from a point source,

  1. the wavefront is spherical.
  2. the intensity decreases in proportion to the distance squared.
  3. the wavefront is parabolic.
  4. the intensity at the wavefront does not depend on the distance.
solution_text: (a) and (b) are correct. Light from point source emits in all around the source with same speed so forms a spherical surface of wavefront or spherical wavefront. As the intensity (I) always decreases as the reciprocal of the square of the distance. {tex}I=\frac{P}{4 \pi r^{2}}{/tex} r = radius of spherical wavefront at any time (r = vt)
solution_html:
(a) and (b) are correct.
Light from point source emits in all around the source with same speed so forms a spherical surface of wavefront or spherical wavefront.

As the intensity (I) always decreases as the reciprocal of the square of the distance.
{tex}I=\frac{P}{4 \pi r^{2}}{/tex}
r = radius of spherical wavefront at any time (r = vt)

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Is Huygen’s principle valid for longitudinal sound waves?
question_html: Is Huygen’s principle valid for longitudinal sound waves?
solution_text: Consider a source of sound formed with the compressions and rarefactions forward in all directions with same velocity. So longitudinal waves propagate with spherical symmetry in all directions as the wavefront in light waves. So Huygen’s principle is valid for longitudinal sound waves also.
solution_html: Consider a source of sound formed with the compressions and rarefactions forward in all directions with same velocity. So longitudinal waves propagate with spherical symmetry in all directions as the wavefront in light waves. So Huygen’s principle is valid for longitudinal sound waves also.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Consider a point at the focal point of a convergent lens. Another convergent lens of short focal length is placed on the other side. What is the nature of the wavefronts emerging from the final image?
question_html: Consider a point at the focal point of a convergent lens. Another convergent lens of short focal length is placed on the other side. What is the nature of the wavefronts emerging from the final image?
solution_text: Consider a point ‘F 1 ’ on focus of converging lens L 1 . The light rays from F 1 , becomes parallel after refraction through L 1 . When these parallel rays falls on converging lens L 2 placed co-axial on the other side of F 1 of L 1 . L 2 converges the rays at it’s focus at I. It now behave like a point source of rays and form a spherical wave front.
solution_html: Consider a point ‘F1’ on focus of converging lens L1. The light rays from F1, becomes parallel after refraction through L1. When these parallel rays falls on converging lens L2 placed co-axial on the other side of F1 of L1. L2 converges the rays at it’s focus at I. It now behave like a point source of rays and form a spherical wave front.

book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: What is the shape of the wavefront on earth for sunlight?
question_html: What is the shape of the wavefront on earth for sunlight?
solution_text: As the sun is very-very far from the earth so can be considered at infinity and sun can be considered as a point source which gives spherical wavefront. The size of the earth is very small as compared to distance of sun from earth and size of the sun so the plane wavefront reaches on earth as shown in figure here.
solution_html: As the sun is very-very far from the earth so can be considered at infinity and sun can be considered as a point source which gives spherical wavefront. The size of the earth is very small as compared to distance of sun from earth and size of the sun so the plane wavefront reaches on earth as shown in figure here.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why is the diffraction of sound waves more evident in daily experience than that of light wave?
question_html: Why is the diffraction of sound waves more evident in daily experience than that of light wave?
solution_text: We know that frequencies of sound waves varies from 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz, so its corresponding wavelength varies from 15m to 15mm respectively. The size of slit (almost) becomes comparable to wavelength of sound so diffraction of sound wave takes place easily. But the wavelength of visible light varies from 0.4 to 0.7 micron which is very small. So the size of most of the slits in not comparable with wavelength of visible light, due to this diffraction of light cannot take place.
solution_html:
We know that frequencies of sound waves varies from 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz, so its corresponding wavelength varies from 15m to 15mm respectively. The size of slit (almost) becomes comparable to wavelength of sound so diffraction of sound wave takes place easily.
But the wavelength of visible light varies from 0.4 to 0.7 micron which is very small. So the size of most of the slits in not comparable with wavelength of visible light, due to this diffraction of light cannot take place.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The human eye has an approximate angular resolution of {tex}\phi{/tex} = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –4 rad and a typical photo printer prints a minimum of 300 dpi (dots per inch, 1 inch = 2.54 cm). At what minimal distance z should a printed page be held so that one does not see the individual dots.
question_html: The human eye has an approximate angular resolution of {tex}\phi{/tex} = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4 rad and a typical photo printer prints a minimum of 300 dpi (dots per inch, 1 inch = 2.54 cm). At what minimal distance z should a printed page be held so that one does not see the individual dots.
solution_text: Angular separation = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 radian The average distance between any two dots = {tex}\frac{2.54}{300}=0.85 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} cm At the distance z cm, angle subtended = {tex}\frac{\operatorname{arc}}{\operatorname{rad}}=\frac{0.85 \times 10^{-2}}{z}{/tex} Resolution angle for human = {tex}5.8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{rad}=\frac{0.85 \times 10^{-2}}{z}{/tex} Maximum distance up to which human eye cannot see 2 dots distinctly {tex}=z=\frac{0.85 \times 10^{-2}}{5.8 \times 10^{-2}}=14.5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} Which is less than distance of distinct vision. So a normal person cannot see the dots.
solution_html:
Angular separation = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 radian
The average distance between any two dots = {tex}\frac{2.54}{300}=0.85 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} cm
At the distance z cm, angle subtended = {tex}\frac{\operatorname{arc}}{\operatorname{rad}}=\frac{0.85 \times 10^{-2}}{z}{/tex}
Resolution angle for human = {tex}5.8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{rad}=\frac{0.85 \times 10^{-2}}{z}{/tex}
Maximum distance up to which human eye cannot see
2 dots distinctly {tex}=z=\frac{0.85 \times 10^{-2}}{5.8 \times 10^{-2}}=14.5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
Which is less than distance of distinct vision.
So a normal person cannot see the dots.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A polaroid (I) is placed in front of a monochromatic source. Another polaroid (II) is placed in front of this polaroid (I) and rotated till no light passes. A third polaroid (III) is now placed in between (I) and (II). In this case, will light emerge from (II). Explain.
question_html: A polaroid (I) is placed in front of a monochromatic source. Another polaroid (II) is placed in front of this polaroid (I) and rotated till no light passes. A third polaroid (III) is now placed in between (I) and (II). In this case, will light emerge from (II). Explain.
solution_text: Polaroid III is placed between two crossed polaroid’s I and II and no light passes through IInd Polaroid. Polaroid III is now placed between I, II and now retakes keeping I, II in no rotation. Let angle between Polaroid I and III be {tex}\theta{/tex} and intensity of plane polarize light after 1 st Polaroid is I 0 . Then I 1 = I 0 I 3 = I 0 cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} is intensity of light after Polaroid III {tex}I_{2}=I_{3} \cos ^{2} \theta^{\prime}=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta \cdot \cos ^{2}(90-\theta){/tex} {tex}I_{2}=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta \cdot \sin ^{2} \theta{/tex} {tex}I_{2}=I_{0}(2 \cos \theta \sin \theta)^{2}=I_{0}(\sin 2 \theta)^{2}{/tex} {tex}I_{2}=\frac{I_{0}}{4} \sin ^{2} 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} is angle between I and III Polaroid. No light will pass through II Polaroid from above eqn. When Sin 2 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 Sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 Sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 Sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin 0 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} No light will pass when angle between I and III Polaroid is zero i.e., plane of Polaroid area parallel . In all other cases when III is not parallel to either I or II, right will pass.
solution_html:
Polaroid III is placed between two crossed polaroid’s I and II and no light passes through IInd Polaroid.
Polaroid III is now placed between I, II and now retakes keeping I, II in no rotation. Let angle between Polaroid I and III be {tex}\theta{/tex} and intensity of plane polarize light after 1st Polaroid is I0. Then I1 = I0

I3 = I0cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} is intensity of light after Polaroid III
{tex}I_{2}=I_{3} \cos ^{2} \theta^{\prime}=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta \cdot \cos ^{2}(90-\theta){/tex}
{tex}I_{2}=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta \cdot \sin ^{2} \theta{/tex}
{tex}I_{2}=I_{0}(2 \cos \theta \sin \theta)^{2}=I_{0}(\sin 2 \theta)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}I_{2}=\frac{I_{0}}{4} \sin ^{2} 2 \theta{/tex}

{tex}\theta{/tex} is angle between I and III Polaroid.
No light will pass through II Polaroid from above eqn. When
Sin2 2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
Sin 2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
Sin 2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
Sin 2{tex}\theta{/tex} = sin 0
{tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No light will pass when angle between I and III Polaroid is zero i.e., plane of Polaroid area parallel . In all other cases when III is not parallel to either I or II, right will pass.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Can reflection result in plane polarized light if the light is incident on the interface from the side with higher refractive index?
question_html: Can reflection result in plane polarized light if the light is incident on the interface from the side with higher refractive index?
solution_text: When the angle of incidence is equal to Brewster's angle, he transmitted light is unpolarised, and reflected light is plane polarised. Consider the diagram in which unpolarised light is represented by dot and plane polarised light is represented by arrows. Polarization by reflection occurs when the angle of incidence is the Brewster's angle, thus tan i B = {tex}^{1} \mu_{2}=\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mu_{1}}{/tex} where {tex}\mu_{2}<\mu_{1}{/tex} when the light rays travel in such a medium, the critical angle is given by, {tex}\sin i_{C}=\frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}{/tex} where {tex}\mu_{2}<\mu_{1}{/tex} As {tex}\left|\tan i_{B}\right|>\left|\sin i_{C}\right|{/tex} for large angles i B < i C Thus, polarization by reflection shall definitely occur.
solution_html:
When the angle of incidence is equal to Brewster's angle, he transmitted light is unpolarised, and reflected light is plane polarised. Consider the diagram in which unpolarised light is represented by dot and plane polarised light is represented by arrows.

Polarization by reflection occurs when the angle of incidence is the Brewster's angle, thus
 tan iB = {tex}^{1} \mu_{2}=\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mu_{1}}{/tex} where {tex}\mu_{2}<\mu_{1}{/tex}
when the light rays travel in such a medium, the critical angle is given by,
{tex}\sin i_{C}=\frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}{/tex}
where {tex}\mu_{2}<\mu_{1}{/tex}
As {tex}\left|\tan i_{B}\right|>\left|\sin i_{C}\right|{/tex} for large angles iB < iC
Thus, polarization by reflection shall definitely occur.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: For the same objective, find the ratio of the least separation between two points to be distinguished by a microscope for light of 5000 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} and electrons accelerated through 100V used as the illuminating substance.
question_html: For the same objective, find the ratio of the least separation between two points to be distinguished by a microscope for light of 5000{tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} and electrons accelerated through 100V used as the illuminating substance.
solution_text: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5000 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} = 5000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m In microscope, {tex}R \cdot P \cdot=\frac{1}{d}=\frac{2 \sin \beta}{1.22 \lambda}{/tex} Limit of resolution by light of 5000 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} {tex}d_{\min }=\frac{1.22 \lambda}{2 \sin \beta} {/tex} or {tex}d_{\min }=\frac{1.22 \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \sin \beta}{/tex} The de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda_d{/tex} of illuminated light = {tex}\frac{1 2.2}{\sqrt{V}}{/tex} nm {tex}\lambda_{d}=\frac{12.2}{\sqrt{100}} n m=1.22\times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}{/tex} The limit of resolution by 100 V light {tex}d_{\min }^{\prime}=\frac{1.22 \lambda_{d}}{2 \sin \beta}{/tex} {tex}d_{\min }^{\prime}=\frac{1.22 \times 1.22 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \sin \beta}{/tex} The required ratio = {tex}\frac{d_{\min }}{d_{\min }}=\frac{\frac{1.22 \times 1.22 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \sin \beta}}{\frac{1.22 \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \sin \beta}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.22}{5000}{/tex} The required ratio = {tex}\frac{122}{500} \times 10^{-3}=0.244 \times 10^{-3}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5000 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} = 5000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m
In microscope, {tex}R \cdot P \cdot=\frac{1}{d}=\frac{2 \sin \beta}{1.22 \lambda}{/tex}
Limit of resolution by light of 5000{tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}
{tex}d_{\min }=\frac{1.22 \lambda}{2 \sin \beta} {/tex} or {tex}d_{\min }=\frac{1.22 \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \sin \beta}{/tex}
The de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda_d{/tex} of illuminated light = {tex}\frac{1 2.2}{\sqrt{V}}{/tex} nm
{tex}\lambda_{d}=\frac{12.2}{\sqrt{100}} n m=1.22\times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
The limit of resolution by 100 V light {tex}d_{\min }^{\prime}=\frac{1.22 \lambda_{d}}{2 \sin \beta}{/tex}
{tex}d_{\min }^{\prime}=\frac{1.22 \times 1.22 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \sin \beta}{/tex}
The required ratio = {tex}\frac{d_{\min }}{d_{\min }}=\frac{\frac{1.22 \times 1.22 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \sin \beta}}{\frac{1.22 \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \sin \beta}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.22}{5000}{/tex}
The required ratio = {tex}\frac{122}{500} \times 10^{-3}=0.244 \times 10^{-3}{/tex}

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Consider a two-slit interference arrangements (Figure) such that the distance of the screen from the slits is half the distance between the slits. Obtain the value of D in terms of λ such that the first minima on the screen fall at a distance D from the center O.
question_html: Consider a two-slit interference arrangements (Figure) such that the distance of the screen from the slits is half the distance between the slits. Obtain the value of D in terms of λ such that the first minima on the screen fall at a distance D from the center O.
solution_text: According to {tex}\theta{/tex} d = D (Given) ...(i) {tex}D=\frac{1}{2} d{/tex} (Given) ...(ii) d = 2D Path difference at P = S 2 P - S 1 P Path difference {tex}p=\sqrt{D^{2}+\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}}-\sqrt{D^{2}+\left(x-\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Substitute the value of d and x from (i) and (ii) {tex}=\sqrt{\left.D^{2}+(D)+D\right)^{2}}-\sqrt{D^{2}+(D-D)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{5 D^{2}}-\sqrt{D^{2}}{/tex} {tex}p=D(\sqrt{5}-1){/tex} The path difference for nth dark fringe from central maxima O is {tex}(2 n-1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} For 1st minima {tex}p=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} Put the value of p in (iii) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}=D(\sqrt{5}-1){/tex} {tex}D=\frac{\lambda}{2(\sqrt{5}-1)}{/tex} Rationalizing the denominator, we get, {tex}D=\frac{\lambda}{2(\sqrt{5}-1)} \times \frac{(\sqrt{5}+1)}{(\sqrt{5}+1)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(2.236+1)}{2 \times(5-1)} \lambda=\frac{3.236}{2 \times 4} \lambda{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3.236}{8} \lambda=0.404 \lambda{/tex}
solution_html:
According to {tex}\theta{/tex}
d = D (Given) ...(i)
{tex}D=\frac{1}{2} d{/tex} (Given) ...(ii)
d = 2D
Path difference at P = S2P - S1P
Path difference {tex}p=\sqrt{D^{2}+\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}}-\sqrt{D^{2}+\left(x-\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
Substitute the value of d and x from (i) and (ii)
{tex}=\sqrt{\left.D^{2}+(D)+D\right)^{2}}-\sqrt{D^{2}+(D-D)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{5 D^{2}}-\sqrt{D^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}p=D(\sqrt{5}-1){/tex}
The path difference for nth dark fringe from central maxima O is {tex}(2 n-1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For 1st minima {tex}p=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
Put the value of p in (iii)
{tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}=D(\sqrt{5}-1){/tex}
{tex}D=\frac{\lambda}{2(\sqrt{5}-1)}{/tex}
Rationalizing the denominator, we get,
{tex}D=\frac{\lambda}{2(\sqrt{5}-1)} \times \frac{(\sqrt{5}+1)}{(\sqrt{5}+1)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{(2.236+1)}{2 \times(5-1)} \lambda=\frac{3.236}{2 \times 4} \lambda{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3.236}{8} \lambda=0.404 \lambda{/tex}

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The figure shows a two-slit arrangement with a source which emits unpolarized light. P is a polarizer with an axis whose direction is not given. If I 0 is the intensity of the principal maxima when no polarizer is present, calculate in the present case, the intensity of the principal maxima as well as of the first minima.
question_html: The figure shows a two-slit arrangement with a source which emits unpolarized light. P is a polarizer with an axis whose direction is not given. If I0 is the intensity of the principal maxima when no polarizer is present, calculate in the present case, the intensity of the principal maxima as well as of the first minima.
solution_text: it is shown in the figure, a two-slit arrangement with a source which emits unpolarized light. Without P {tex}\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{A}_{\perp}+\mathrm{A}_{11}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{A}_{\perp}=\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{1}+\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{2}=\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\sin (k x-\omega t)+\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{\circ} \sin (k x-\omega t+\phi){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{A}_{11}=\mathrm{A}_{11}^{(1)}+\mathrm{A}_{11}^{(2)}{/tex} {tex}A_{11}=A_{11}^{0}[\sin (k x-\omega t)+\sin (k x-\omega t+\phi)]{/tex} where {tex}\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{0}, \mathrm{A}_{11}^{0}{/tex} are the amplitudes of either of the beam in {tex}\perp{/tex} and 11 polarizations. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity {tex}=\left\{\left|A_{1}^{0}\right|^{2}+\left|A_{11}^{0}\right|^{2}\right\}\left[\sin ^{2}(k x-\omega t)\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\left(1+\cos ^{2} \phi+2 \sin \phi\right)+\sin ^{2}(k x-\omega t) \sin ^{2} \phi\right]_{\text {average }}{/tex} {tex}=\left\{\left|A_{1}^{0}\right|^{2}+\left|A_{11}^{0}\right|^{2}\right\}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \cdot 2(1+\cos \phi){/tex} {tex}=2\left|A_{1}^{0}\right|^{2} \cdot(1+\cos \phi) \operatorname{since}\left|A_{1}^{0}\right|_{average {} e}=\left|A_{11}^{0}\right|_{average}{/tex} With P Assume {tex}A_{\perp}^{2}{/tex} is blocked; Intensity {tex}=\left(\mathrm{A}_{11}^{1}+\mathrm{A}_{11}^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{1}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\left|A_{\perp}^{0}\right|^{2}(1+\cos \phi)+\left|A_{\perp}^{0}\right|^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Given; {tex}I_{o}=4\left|\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{\circ}\right|^{2}{/tex} = Intensity without polarizer at principal maxima. Intensity at principal maxima with a polarizer {tex}=\left|\mathbf{A}_{\perp}^{\circ}\right|^{2}\left(2+\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{8} I_{0}{/tex} Intensity at first minima with a polarizer {tex}=\left|A_{\perp}^{0}\right|^{2}(1-1)+\frac{\left|A_{\perp}^{0}\right|^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{I_{0}}{8}{/tex}
solution_html:
it is shown in the figure, a two-slit arrangement with a source which emits unpolarized light.
Without P
{tex}\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{A}_{\perp}+\mathrm{A}_{11}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{A}_{\perp}=\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{1}+\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{2}=\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{\circ}{/tex}{tex}\sin (k x-\omega t)+\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{\circ} \sin (k x-\omega t+\phi){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{A}_{11}=\mathrm{A}_{11}^{(1)}+\mathrm{A}_{11}^{(2)}{/tex}
{tex}A_{11}=A_{11}^{0}[\sin (k x-\omega t)+\sin (k x-\omega t+\phi)]{/tex}
where {tex}\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{0}, \mathrm{A}_{11}^{0}{/tex}
are the amplitudes of either of the beam in {tex}\perp{/tex} and 11 polarizations.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity {tex}=\left\{\left|A_{1}^{0}\right|^{2}+\left|A_{11}^{0}\right|^{2}\right\}\left[\sin ^{2}(k x-\omega t)\right.{/tex}{tex}\left.\left(1+\cos ^{2} \phi+2 \sin \phi\right)+\sin ^{2}(k x-\omega t) \sin ^{2} \phi\right]_{\text {average }}{/tex}
{tex}=\left\{\left|A_{1}^{0}\right|^{2}+\left|A_{11}^{0}\right|^{2}\right\}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \cdot 2(1+\cos \phi){/tex}
{tex}=2\left|A_{1}^{0}\right|^{2} \cdot(1+\cos \phi) \operatorname{since}\left|A_{1}^{0}\right|_{average {} e}=\left|A_{11}^{0}\right|_{average}{/tex}
With P
Assume {tex}A_{\perp}^{2}{/tex} is blocked;
Intensity {tex}=\left(\mathrm{A}_{11}^{1}+\mathrm{A}_{11}^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{1}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\left|A_{\perp}^{0}\right|^{2}(1+\cos \phi)+\left|A_{\perp}^{0}\right|^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Given; {tex}I_{o}=4\left|\mathrm{A}_{\perp}^{\circ}\right|^{2}{/tex}
= Intensity without polarizer at principal maxima.
Intensity at principal maxima with a polarizer
{tex}=\left|\mathbf{A}_{\perp}^{\circ}\right|^{2}\left(2+\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5}{8} I_{0}{/tex}
Intensity at first minima with a polarizer
{tex}=\left|A_{\perp}^{0}\right|^{2}(1-1)+\frac{\left|A_{\perp}^{0}\right|^{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{I_{0}}{8}{/tex}

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A small transparent slab containing material of {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 is placed along AS 2 (Figure). What will be the distance from O of the principal maxima and of the first minima on either side of the principal maxima obtained in the absence of the glass slab? AC = CO = D, S 1 C = S 2 C = d << D
question_html:
A small transparent slab containing material of {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 is placed along AS2 (Figure). What will be the distance from O of the principal maxima and of the first minima on either side of the principal maxima obtained in the absence of the glass slab?

AC = CO = D, S1C = S2C = d << D
solution_text: As is clear from figure and difference between waves reaching P 1 from A is {tex}=2 d \sin \theta+(u-1) l{/tex} For principal maximum, path difference = 0 i.e., {tex}2 d \sin \theta+(\mu-1) l=0{/tex} 2d {tex}=\sin \theta+(1.5-1) \frac{d}{4}=0, \sin \theta=\frac{-1}{16}{/tex} {tex}\therefore O P_{1}=(C O) \tan \theta \cong D\left(-\frac{1}{16}\right){/tex} For the first minimum, an angle {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex} , say, path difference {tex}=2 d \sin \theta_{1}+0.5 l=\pm \lambda / 2{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta_{1}=\frac{\pm \lambda / 2-0.5 l}{2 d}{/tex} As diffraction occurs when d = {tex}\lambda{/tex} , {tex}\therefore \sin \theta_{1}=\frac{\pm \lambda / 2-\lambda / 8}{2 \lambda}=\pm \frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}{/tex} on the positive side, {tex}\sin \theta_{1}=+\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}=\frac{3}{16}{/tex} , on the negative side, {tex}\sin \theta_{1}^{\prime}=-\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}=\frac{-5}{16}{/tex} The first principal maximum on the positive side is at distance (above O) = D than {tex} \theta_{1}=D \frac{\sin \theta_{1}}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta_{1}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{D .3 / 16}{\sqrt{1}-9 / 256}=\frac{3 D}{\sqrt{16^{2}-3^{2}}}{/tex} On the negative side, the distance of first principal maximum (below O) will be {tex}=D \theta_{1}^{\prime}=D \frac{\sin \theta_{1}^{\prime}}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta_{1}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{D(-5 / 16)}{\sqrt{1-(5 / 16)^{2}}}=\frac{-5 D}{\sqrt{16^{2}-5^{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
As is clear from figure and difference between waves reaching P1 from A is 
{tex}=2 d \sin \theta+(u-1) l{/tex}
For principal maximum, path difference = 0
i.e., {tex}2 d \sin \theta+(\mu-1) l=0{/tex}
2d {tex}=\sin \theta+(1.5-1) \frac{d}{4}=0, \sin \theta=\frac{-1}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore O P_{1}=(C O) \tan \theta \cong D\left(-\frac{1}{16}\right){/tex}
For the first minimum, an angle {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex}, say,
path difference {tex}=2 d \sin \theta_{1}+0.5 l=\pm \lambda / 2{/tex}
{tex}\sin \theta_{1}=\frac{\pm \lambda / 2-0.5 l}{2 d}{/tex}
As diffraction occurs when d = {tex}\lambda{/tex},
{tex}\therefore \sin \theta_{1}=\frac{\pm \lambda / 2-\lambda / 8}{2 \lambda}=\pm \frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}{/tex}
on the positive side, {tex}\sin \theta_{1}=+\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}=\frac{3}{16}{/tex}, on the negative side,
{tex}\sin \theta_{1}^{\prime}=-\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}=\frac{-5}{16}{/tex}
The first principal maximum on the positive side is at distance (above O)
= D than {tex} \theta_{1}=D \frac{\sin \theta_{1}}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta_{1}}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{D .3 / 16}{\sqrt{1}-9 / 256}=\frac{3 D}{\sqrt{16^{2}-3^{2}}}{/tex}
On the negative side, the distance of first principal maximum (below O) will be
{tex}=D \theta_{1}^{\prime}=D \frac{\sin \theta_{1}^{\prime}}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta_{1}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{D(-5 / 16)}{\sqrt{1-(5 / 16)^{2}}}=\frac{-5 D}{\sqrt{16^{2}-5^{2}}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Four identical monochromatic sources A, B, C, D as shown in the (Figure) produce waves of the same wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} and are coherent. Two receiver R 1 and R 2 are at great but equal distances from B. Which of the two receivers picks up the larger signal? Which of the two receivers picks up the larger signal when B is turned off? Which of the two receivers picks up the larger signal when D is turned off? Which of the two receivers can distinguish which of the sources B or D has been turned off?
question_html:
Four identical monochromatic sources A, B, C, D as shown in the (Figure) produce waves of the same wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} and are coherent. Two receiver R1 and R2 are at great but equal distances from B.

  1. Which of the two receivers picks up the larger signal?
  2. Which of the two receivers picks up the larger signal when B is turned off?
  3. Which of the two receivers picks up the larger signal when D is turned off?
  4. Which of the two receivers can distinguish which of the sources B or D has been turned off?
solution_text: Consider the disturbances at the receiver R 1 which is at a distance d from B. Let the wave at R 1 because of A be y A {tex}a \cos \omega t{/tex} . The path difference of the signal from A with from B is {tex}\frac {\lambda}{2}{/tex} and hence, the phase difference is {tex}\pi{/tex} Thus, the wave at R 1 because of B is {tex}y_{B}=a \cos (\omega t-\pi)=-a \cos \omega t{/tex} The path difference of the signal from C with that from A is {tex}\lambda{/tex} and hence the phase difference is 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} Thus, the wave at R 1 because of C is y C {tex}=a \cos (\omega t-2 \pi)=a \cos \omega t{/tex} The path difference between the signal from D with that of A is {tex}\sqrt{d^{2}+\left(\frac{\lambda^{2}}{2}\right)-(d- \frac \lambda 2)}{/tex} {tex}=d\left(1+\frac{\lambda}{4 d^{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}-d+\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}=d\left(1+\frac{\lambda}{4 d^{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}-d+\frac{\lambda}{2} \approx \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}(\therefore d>>\lambda){/tex} Therefore, the phase difference is {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore Y_{D}=\operatorname{acos}(\omega t-\pi)=-a \cos \omega t{/tex} Thus, the signal picked up at R 1 from all the four sources is {tex}Y_{R_{1}}=y_{A}+y_{B}+y_{C}+y_{D}{/tex} {tex}=a \cos \omega t-a \cos \omega t+a \cos \omega t-a \cos \omega t=0{/tex} Let the signal picked up at R 2 from B be Y B {tex}=a_{1} \cos \omega t{/tex} The path difference between signal at D and that at B is {tex}\frac {\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}\because y_{D}=-a_{1} \cos \omega t{/tex} The path difference between signal at A and that at B is {tex}\sqrt{d^{2}+\left(\frac{\lambda^{2}}{2}\right)}-d{/tex} {tex}=d\left(1+\frac{\lambda^{2}}{4 d^{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}-d \quad \sim \frac{1 \lambda^{2}}{8 d^{2}}{/tex} As d >> {tex}\lambda{/tex} , therefore this path difference {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0 and phase difference {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\left(\frac{1}{8} \frac{\lambda^{2}}{d^{2}}\right) \rightarrow 0{/tex} Hence, y A {tex}=a_{1} \cos (\omega t-\phi){/tex} Similarly, {tex}y_{C}=a_{1} \cos (\omega t-\phi){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Signal picked up by R 2 is y A + y B + y C + y D {tex}=y=2 a_{1} \cos (\omega t-\phi){/tex} {tex}\left.\therefore y\right|^{2}=4 a_{1}^{2} \cos ^{2}(\omega t-\phi){/tex} {tex}\therefore<I>=2 a_{1}^{2}{/tex} Thus, R 1 picks up the larger isgnal. If B is switched off, R 1 picks up y = acos {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}\left\langle I_{R_{1}}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2} a^{2}{/tex} R 2 pick up y {tex}=a \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore\left\langle I_{R_{2}}\right\rangle=a^{2}<\cos ^{2} \omega t>y=\frac{a^{2}}{2}{/tex} Thus, R 1 and R 2 pick up the same signal If D is switched off. R 1 pick up y {tex}=a \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore\left\langle I_{R_{1}}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2} a^{2}{/tex} R 2 picks up y {tex}=a \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore\left\langle I_{R_{2}}\right\rangle=9 a^{2}<\cos ^{2} \omega t>y=\frac{9 a^{2}}{2}{/tex} Thus, R 2 picks up larger signal compared to R 1 . Thus, a signal at R1 indicates B has been switched off and an enhanced signal at R 2 indicates D has been switched off.
solution_html:
Consider the disturbances at the receiver R1 which is at a distance d from B.
Let the wave at R1 because of A be yA {tex}a \cos \omega t{/tex}. The path difference of the signal from A with from B is {tex}\frac {\lambda}{2}{/tex} and hence, the phase difference is {tex}\pi{/tex}
Thus, the wave at R1 because of B is
{tex}y_{B}=a \cos (\omega t-\pi)=-a \cos \omega t{/tex}
The path difference of the signal from C with that from A is {tex}\lambda{/tex} and hence the phase difference is 2{tex}\pi{/tex}
Thus, the wave at R1 because of C is yC {tex}=a \cos (\omega t-2 \pi)=a \cos \omega t{/tex}

The path difference between the signal from D with that of A is
{tex}\sqrt{d^{2}+\left(\frac{\lambda^{2}}{2}\right)-(d- \frac \lambda 2)}{/tex} {tex}=d\left(1+\frac{\lambda}{4 d^{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}-d+\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=d\left(1+\frac{\lambda}{4 d^{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}-d+\frac{\lambda}{2} \approx \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}(\therefore d>>\lambda){/tex}
Therefore, the phase difference is {tex}\pi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore Y_{D}=\operatorname{acos}(\omega t-\pi)=-a \cos \omega t{/tex}
Thus, the signal picked up at R1 from all the four sources is {tex}Y_{R_{1}}=y_{A}+y_{B}+y_{C}+y_{D}{/tex}
{tex}=a \cos \omega t-a \cos \omega t+a \cos \omega t-a \cos \omega t=0{/tex}

  1. Let the signal picked up at R2 from B be YB {tex}=a_{1} \cos \omega t{/tex}
    The path difference between signal at D and that at B is {tex}\frac {\lambda}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\because y_{D}=-a_{1} \cos \omega t{/tex}
    The path difference between signal at A and that at B is
    {tex}\sqrt{d^{2}+\left(\frac{\lambda^{2}}{2}\right)}-d{/tex} {tex}=d\left(1+\frac{\lambda^{2}}{4 d^{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}-d \quad \sim \frac{1 \lambda^{2}}{8 d^{2}}{/tex}
    As d >> {tex}\lambda{/tex}, therefore this path difference {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0
    and phase difference {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\left(\frac{1}{8} \frac{\lambda^{2}}{d^{2}}\right) \rightarrow 0{/tex}
    Hence, yA {tex}=a_{1} \cos (\omega t-\phi){/tex}
    Similarly, {tex}y_{C}=a_{1} \cos (\omega t-\phi){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Signal picked up by R2 is
    yA + yB + yC + yD {tex}=y=2 a_{1} \cos (\omega t-\phi){/tex}
    {tex}\left.\therefore y\right|^{2}=4 a_{1}^{2} \cos ^{2}(\omega t-\phi){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore<I>=2 a_{1}^{2}{/tex}
    Thus, R1 picks up the larger isgnal.
  2. If B is switched off,
    R1 picks up y = acos {tex}\omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\left\langle I_{R_{1}}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2} a^{2}{/tex}
    R2 pick up y {tex}=a \cos \omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore\left\langle I_{R_{2}}\right\rangle=a^{2}<\cos ^{2} \omega t>y=\frac{a^{2}}{2}{/tex}
  3. Thus, R1 and R2 pick up the same signal
    If D is switched off.
    R1 pick up y {tex}=a \cos \omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore\left\langle I_{R_{1}}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2} a^{2}{/tex}
    R2 picks up y {tex}=a \cos \omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore\left\langle I_{R_{2}}\right\rangle=9 a^{2}<\cos ^{2} \omega t>y=\frac{9 a^{2}}{2}{/tex}
    Thus, R2 picks up larger signal compared to R1.
  4. Thus, a signal at R1 indicates B has been switched off and an enhanced signal at R2 indicates D has been switched off.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The optical properties of a medium are governed by the relative permittivity {tex}\left(\varepsilon_{r}\right){/tex} and relative permeability {tex}\left(\mu_{r}\right){/tex} . The refractive index is defined as {tex}\sqrt{\mu_{r} \varepsilon_{r}}=n{/tex} . For ordinary material {tex}\varepsilon_{r}>0{/tex} and {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{r}}>0{/tex} and the positive sign is taken for the square root. In 1964, a Russian scientist V. Veselago postulated the existence of material with {tex}\varepsilon_{r}{/tex} < 0 and {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{r}}<0{/tex} . Since then such ‘metamaterials’ have been produced in the laboratories and their optical properties studied. For such materials {tex}n=-\sqrt{\mu_{r} \varepsilon_{r}}{/tex} . As light enters a medium of such a refractive index the phases travel away from the direction of propagation. According to the description above show that if rays of light enter such a medium from the air (refractive index =1) at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} in 2nd quadrant, then the refracted beam is in the 3rd quadrant. Prove that Snell’s law holds for such a medium.
question_html:
The optical properties of a medium are governed by the relative permittivity {tex}\left(\varepsilon_{r}\right){/tex} and relative permeability {tex}\left(\mu_{r}\right){/tex}. The refractive index is defined as {tex}\sqrt{\mu_{r} \varepsilon_{r}}=n{/tex}. For ordinary material {tex}\varepsilon_{r}>0{/tex} and {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{r}}>0{/tex} and the positive sign is taken for the square root. In 1964, a Russian scientist V. Veselago postulated the existence of material with {tex}\varepsilon_{r}{/tex} < 0 and {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{r}}<0{/tex}. Since then such ‘metamaterials’ have been produced in the laboratories and their optical properties studied. For such materials {tex}n=-\sqrt{\mu_{r} \varepsilon_{r}}{/tex}. As light enters a medium of such a refractive index the phases travel away from the direction of propagation.

  1. According to the description above show that if rays of light enter such a medium from the air (refractive index =1) at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} in 2nd quadrant, then the refracted beam is in the 3rd quadrant.
  2. Prove that Snell’s law holds for such a medium.
solution_text: Suppose the postulate is true, then two parallel rays would proceed as shown in Fig (1). Assuming ED shows a wave front then all points on this must have the same phase. All points with the same optical path length must have the same phase. Thus {tex}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}} A E=\mathrm{BC}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}} \mathrm{CD}{/tex} or {tex}B C=\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}}(C D-A E){/tex} BC > 0, CD > AE As showing that the postulate is reasonable. If however, the light proceeded in the sense it does for ordinary material (viz. in the fourth quadrant, Fig. 2) Then {tex}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}} A E=\mathrm{B} \mathrm{C}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}} \mathrm{CD}{/tex} or {tex}B C=\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}}(C D-A E){/tex} As AE > CD, BC < 0 Showing that this is not possible. Hence the postulate is correct. From figure (1) BC = AC sin {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex} and CD - AE = AC sin {tex}\theta_{\mathrm{r}}{/tex} : Since {tex}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}}(A E-C D)=B C{/tex} {tex}-n \sin \theta_{\mathrm{r}}=\sin \theta_{c}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Suppose the postulate is true, then two parallel rays would proceed as shown in Fig (1). Assuming ED shows a wave front then all points on this must have the same phase. All points with the same optical path length must have the same phase.
    Thus {tex}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}} A E=\mathrm{BC}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}} \mathrm{CD}{/tex}
    or {tex}B C=\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}}(C D-A E){/tex}
    BC > 0, CD > AE
    As showing that the postulate is reasonable. If however, the light proceeded in the sense it does for ordinary material (viz. in the fourth quadrant, Fig. 2)
    Then {tex}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}} A E=\mathrm{B} \mathrm{C}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}} \mathrm{CD}{/tex}
    or {tex}B C=\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}}(C D-A E){/tex}
    As AE > CD, BC < 0
    Showing that this is not possible. Hence the postulate is correct.
  2. From figure (1)
    BC = AC sin {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex} and CD - AE = AC sin {tex}\theta_{\mathrm{r}}{/tex}:
    Since {tex}-\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}}(A E-C D)=B C{/tex}
    {tex}-n \sin \theta_{\mathrm{r}}=\sin \theta_{c}{/tex}

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: To ensure almost 100 percent transmittivity, photographic lenses are often coated with a thin layer of dielectric material. The refractive index of this material is intermediated between that of air and glass (which makes the optical element of the lens). A typically used dielectric film is MgF 2 (n = 1.38). What should the thickness of the film be so that at the center of the visible spectrum (5500 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} ) there is the maximum transmission?
question_html: To ensure almost 100 percent transmittivity, photographic lenses are often coated with a thin layer of dielectric material. The refractive index of this material is intermediated between that of air and glass (which makes the optical element of the lens). A typically used dielectric film is MgF2 (n = 1.38). What should the thickness of the film be so that at the center of the visible spectrum (5500 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}) there is the maximum transmission?
solution_text: Consider a ray incident at an angle i. A part of this ray is reflected from the air-film interface and a part refracted inside. This is partly reflected at the film-glass interface and a part transmitted. A part of the reflected ray is reflected at the film-air interface and a part transmitted as r 2 parallel to r 1 . Of course, successive reflections and transmissions will keep on decreasing the amplitude of the wave. Hence rays r 1 and r 2 shall dominate the behaviour. If the incident light is to be transmitted through the lens, r 1 and r 2 should interfere destructively. Both the reflections at A and D are from lower to higher refractive index and hence there is no phase change on reflection. The optical path difference between r 2 and r 1 is n(AD + CD) - AB. If d is the thickness of the film, then {tex}A D=C D=\frac{d}{\cos r}{/tex} AB = AC sin i {tex}\frac{A C}{2}=d \tan r{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} AC = 2d tan r Hence, AB = 2d tan r {tex}\times{/tex} sin i Thus the optical path difference is given by, {tex}2 n \frac{d}{\cos r}- 2 d \tan r \times \sin i{/tex} {tex}=2 \cdot \frac{\sin i}{\sin r} \cdot \frac{d}{\cos r} - 2 d \cdot \frac{\sin r}{\cos r} \cdot \sin i{/tex} {tex}=2 d \cdot \sin i\left[\frac{1-\sin ^{2} r}{\sin r \cos r}\right]=2 n d \cdot \cos r{/tex} For these waves to interfere destructively this must be {tex}\frac {\lambda}{2}{/tex} . 2nd cos {tex}r=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} to nd cos r {tex}=\frac{\lambda}{4}{/tex} For a camera lens, the sources are in the vertical plane and hence we get; {tex}i \simeq r \simeq 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore n d \simeq \frac{\lambda}{4}{/tex} , hence thickness of the film is given by; {tex} \Rightarrow d = \frac{{5500\mathop A\limits^0 }}{{1.38 \times 4}} \simeq 1000\mathop A\limits^0 {/tex}
solution_html:
Consider a ray incident at an angle i. A part of this ray is reflected from the air-film interface and a part refracted inside. This is partly reflected at the film-glass interface and a part transmitted. A part of the reflected ray is reflected at the film-air interface and a part transmitted as r2 parallel to r1. Of course, successive reflections and transmissions will keep on decreasing the amplitude of the wave. Hence rays r1 and r2 shall dominate the behaviour. If the incident light is to be transmitted through the lens, r1 and r2 should interfere destructively. Both the reflections at A and D are from lower to higher refractive index and hence there is no phase change on reflection. The optical path difference between r2 and r1 is n(AD + CD) - AB.
If d is the thickness of the film, then
{tex}A D=C D=\frac{d}{\cos r}{/tex}
AB = AC sin i
{tex}\frac{A C}{2}=d \tan r{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AC = 2d tan r
Hence, AB = 2d tan r {tex}\times{/tex} sin i
Thus the optical path difference is given by,
{tex}2 n \frac{d}{\cos r}- 2 d \tan r \times \sin i{/tex}
{tex}=2 \cdot \frac{\sin i}{\sin r} \cdot \frac{d}{\cos r} - 2 d \cdot \frac{\sin r}{\cos r} \cdot \sin i{/tex}
{tex}=2 d \cdot \sin i\left[\frac{1-\sin ^{2} r}{\sin r \cos r}\right]=2 n d \cdot \cos r{/tex}
For these waves to interfere destructively this must be {tex}\frac {\lambda}{2}{/tex}.
2nd cos {tex}r=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} to nd cos r {tex}=\frac{\lambda}{4}{/tex}
For a camera lens, the sources are in the vertical plane and hence we get;
{tex}i \simeq r \simeq 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore n d \simeq \frac{\lambda}{4}{/tex} , hence thickness of the film is given by;
{tex} \Rightarrow d = \frac{{5500\mathop A\limits^0 }}{{1.38 \times 4}} \simeq 1000\mathop A\limits^0 {/tex}
